author: @brandonlaird6876
text:
I couldn't believe it at first. My first thought was "there's way too much wiggle room, with a powerful transformer, and there's no way sound is gonna come out."
I sat stunned for about 30 seconds when I heard it. You, sir, are a legend. I've only been following your channel for a couple weeks but absolutely love your take on these kinds of projects. You inspire creativity in simplicity with complex concepts, and I hope that sounds right to you.
Love all your videos so far.

author: @jamest.5001
text: No spider, or anything traditional, but likely not good for mid low range frequency,  I saw a antique radio for sale a while back, it had the strangest speaker, it looked more like a antenna and record player's offspring, it had a pin inside a cone shape paper, inside metal ring, the pin had a coil attached,  some how it made sound through the paper cone! I didn't see a magnet, maybe the pin was a magnet? I just know it was weird! If it wasn't making sound on top of a old radio, I would think it was a antenna, or just be clueless! Things have come a ways since then , but we are not nearly as far from that tech ad we could / should be!!

author: @BonsaiBrandy
text: would this work for crystal radios? i know those speakers need to be high impedance but i can only find one video where someone has actually tried to make them

author: @travismoore7849
text: If it were me I would have use the microwave transformer with he high voltage to drive a quartz crystal. Make sure the wires are on two parallel faces of the crystal. It should act like a piezo speaker. I did this with a direct hook up and it worked but was quiet. So a Large cube or large sheet of Rochelle salt might work as well.

author: @ThomasAndersonbsf
text: a good source of diaphrame material I would think would be the mylar packaging of things like chips (crisps in the UK I believe) or the clear windows from boxes of say bakery items from the bakery section of a grocery store. (I have been saving stuff like that to use as very effective partial wave rectifiers, like I was using in my variable color (rotate to change its color) light filter. :)

author: @ThomasAndersonbsf
text: a good reason for the speaker and tearing it down to do this I believe would be to make things that would be far better tools than just strapping a speaker to something (like I am using a subwoofer for a vibration unit like long term large flake generation of stuff by causing it to knock about against each other and other things with graphite, sort of like a rock tumbler that shakes instead of rolling around, but with controllable frequency of vibrations up to that 20kHz, now if I could make something that can go much much higher and still have the ability to be controlled in a wide range between 1mHz to say 10mHz LOL  anyway my point is modifying the bits (magnet and coil) from a subwoofer instead of this set up I think might be more efficient and allow building ALL sorts of tools, your hacksaw idea I really like as a wobbly bit that has its own rigidity, going to steal that instead of thanks to your video not having to come up with it on my own (I might have but it would have taken time and looking over all my bits and bobs before the sparks flew and I ended up realizing it would work for that, since most of us think of saws for cutting and not about the other properties they have XD) but I was going to say, originally as I was watching I thought about using the coil to cause an in and out action or back and forth action on a rail that you can put an extended bit of abrasive covered item (like a diamond file covered with crushed diamond) and use it as a vibration cutter :) basically an electric saw that has the ability to cut through hard stuff like silicon wafers or quartz and glass XD. (or shape it) always love how your videos get me thinking and coming up with all sorts of new stuff,

author: @stevetobias4890
text:
That was bloody awesome Robert. I wonder if this can be replicated winding your own coil. I guess it would just be a matter of the right number of windings. Iron core isn't too hard a and I have some of that thick black plastic board. I will have to experiment with different coils.
As far as the tinny sound, that can be overcome by a different material for the membrane / diaphragm. Maybe even a different size? I think a really thick and large membrane will give more bass. I am just thinking this due to standard speaker sizes in a stereo setup, subwoofers are big, tweeters are small and normal speakers are medium.
It all comes down t o experimenting I guess.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: tbh mate just about anything will do - optimising it will be harder but getting it working with your own coil would be pretty easy

author: @claytonmccormick622
text: the reason it is tinny is the hacksaw blade is relatively short and favors high frequencies as  its natural resonant frequency . with a frequency divider network  and some careful choices as to size  you could get a subwoofer woofer midrange and tweeters with a resonant cavity for each and some pots to fine tune the balance between frq. ranges i think you would have a very nice speaker  :-)
